explanation: The statement "A DM cannot prop_0 after 2 time units prop_1" means that if prop_1 holds at any given time, then prop_0 must not hold two time units later. This is expressed using a global implication: G (prop_1 -> X X !prop_0).

The statement "prop_0 after 2 time units prop_1" implies a global property where if 'prop_0' occurs, then 'prop_1' must occur exactly two time units later. The 'G' operator signifies that this relationship holds at all times. The '->' operator expresses the implication. The 'X X' operators mean 'next next', indicating two time steps into the future.

The statement "A DM cannot prop_0 after 2 time units prop_1" means that if 'prop_1' occurs at any given time, then 'prop_0' must not occur exactly two time units later. The 'G' operator ensures this condition holds globally. The '->' operator expresses the implication. The 'X' operator, used twice (X(X(...))), denotes the state exactly two time units in the future. Finally, '!' negates 'prop_0' to express that it cannot occur.

The statement "A DM cannot prop_0 after 2 time units prop_1" means that if 'prop_1' occurs at any point in time, then 'prop_0' must be false starting from two time units after 'prop_1' occurred and must remain false thereafter. The 'G' operator at the beginning ensures this condition holds globally. Inside the implication, 'prop_1' is the trigger. 'X X' advances the time by two steps. 'G !prop_0' asserts that 'prop_0' must be globally false from that advanced point onwards.

The statement "A DM cannot prop_0 after 2 time units prop_1" means that if 'prop_1' occurs at some point in time, then 'prop_0' must not occur exactly two time units later. We can express this using the 'Globally' (G) operator to apply this rule at all times. Inside the 'Globally' operator, we use an implication: if 'prop_1' is true, then it must be that 'prop_0' is not true two time steps later. 'X X !prop_0' translates to 'not prop_0' at 'next next' time.

The statement "A DM cannot prop_0 after 2 time units prop_1" implies a global constraint. If 'prop_1' holds at any given time, then 'prop_0' must not hold two time units later. 'G' signifies "globally" (always). '->' signifies implication. 'X' signifies "next" (one time unit later), so 'X X' signifies two time units later. '!prop_0' signifies that 'prop_0' is false.

The statement "A DM cannot prop_0 after 2 time units prop_1" means that if 'prop_1' occurs at any point in time, then 'prop_0' must not occur exactly two time units later. We use 'G' for 'globally' to ensure this holds at all times. The implication 'prop_1 ->' means 'if prop_1 is true now'. 'X X' represents two 'next' time units. Finally, '!prop_0' means 'prop_0 is false'.

The statement "A DM cannot prop_0 after 2 time units it prop_1" means that if 'prop_1' occurs at some point, then 'prop_0' must not occur two time units later. This translates to an LTL formula that universally holds: if 'prop_1' is true, then 'prop_0' must be false in the state after two steps. The 'G' operator ensures this condition holds at all times. 'X X !prop_0' means that 'prop_0' is false in the state that is two steps into the future.

The statement "A DM cannot prop_0 after 2 time units prop_1" means that it is never allowed for prop_1 to be true at a given point in time, and then for prop_0 to be true exactly two time units later. This can be expressed by stating that it is globally false that (prop_1 is true NOW AND prop_0 is true two steps later).

The statement 'A DM cannot prop_0 after 2 time units prop_1' means that if 'prop_1' occurs at any point in time, then 'prop_0' must not occur two time units later. We use 'G' for 'globally' to ensure this property holds at all times. The implication '->' connects 'prop_1' as the trigger with '!prop_0' two steps in the future. 'X X' represents two steps in the future.
